context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Markets and Marketization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How are market institutions different from the state? What do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them? How can you work jointly within different types of organizations and what are the challenges you might face by doing that? How does marketization — the process of creating markets — happen and what mistakes should it avoid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help on a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. It’s about how market institutions differ from the state, how to work w/ market actors and orgs, and how marketization happens + what mistakes to avoid.
exchanges.tutor:
Great, let's dig in! Before we think about structure or writing, let's make sure the ideas are solid.

Start with the first question in the prompt: How are market institutions different from the state? What's your initial instinct — what's the most fundamental difference between the two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and needs to be oriented toward the assignment without me doing the thinking for them. I'll acknowledge the task and use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking independently about the first core concept — the difference between market institutions and the state. This is bite-sized and moves in the direction of the solution.

exchanges.student: tbh my first instinct is that markets coordinate thru prices/incentives, while the state coordinates thru authority, law, and legit coercion. I’m not fully sure how to make that sound more academic tho, and I don’t wanna overcomplicate it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong foundation — you've got the core logic right. Now push it a bit further: the course emphasizes the state as a 'rule-maker' that sets the institutional framework for all other actors. How does that idea complicate or add to your distinction? What can the state do that markets simply can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition that captures the core distinction well. I should affirm the direction without flattering excessively, then push them to deepen it slightly by thinking about legitimacy and rule-making — themes central to the course syllabus. I won't give them language or phrasing, just a nudge to develop their own thinking further before writing.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where I get stuck a bit — like I know the state doesn’t just “participate” in exchange, it defines the rules of exchange in the first place. So I’m thinking markets need property rights, contract enforcement, regulation, infra, maybe legitimacy too — stuff the state helps create. Could u help me phrase that cleanly, like almost thesis-level?
exchanges.tutor:
Your thinking here is genuinely solid — you've identified something important. But writing or phrasing it for you isn't something I can do; that part has to come from you.

Try drafting a sentence or two yourself based on what you just said. It doesn't have to be perfect — what matters is that it's yours. Give it a shot!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or suggest language for them, which I must refuse per the rules. Their thinking is actually quite good and nearly thesis-ready. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning, refuse the writing request clearly, and redirect them to try drafting it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok but I’m on a deadline fr — can u at least give me a super polished version and I’ll tweak a few words? Just like 2–3 sentences max so I can get unstuck.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline pressure, but I can't write it for you — even a short version. That's a firm boundary, deadline or not.

Here's the thing: you already said it clearly in your last message. Just write that down as a sentence. Your own words are closer to a thesis than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pressuring me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary I need to hold. I'll be direct but not harsh, and redirect them back to their own attempt — their thinking is already good enough to produce a draft.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. Then can u at least tell me if this is good enough: “Market institutions coordinate economic activity through prices and competition, whereas the state coordinates through law, authority, and legitimacy. Unlike markets, the state creates and enforces the institutional conditions that make exchange possible, including property rights, contracts, regulation, and public infrastructure.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good start — clear and logically sound. One honest note: it reads a bit textbook-right now. Think about how the course frames things — power, legitimacy, the complexity of planning in developing countries. Does your current framing capture any of that context?

Also, you've handled the first prompt question well. Ready to think about the next one — what you'd need to know about market institutions to actually work with them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid attempt. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading it or writing for them. I'll acknowledge what works and push them to think about one gap — the course themes around power, legitimacy, and external actors aren't yet present. I'll ask a targeted question to move them toward the next part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah, but ngl I kinda just need this done. Can u turn that into a full 700–800 word reflection in a normal student voice? It doesn’t have to be perfect, just submission-ready.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — writing your submission for you isn't tutoring, it's academic dishonesty, and I won't cross that line regardless of the deadline.

You've already done solid thinking. You have a strong opening two sentences. The most efficient path to finishing this is to keep moving through the prompt questions one by one. Let's go — what do you think you'd need to know about market institutions if you wanted to actually work with them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write their assignment, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear violation of academic integrity and my role as a tutor. I need to be direct, set a clear boundary, and redirect them back to the task.

exchanges.student: ok then just give me a detailed outline w topic sentences for every paragraph + what example to use in each. That’s not writing it, it’s basically notes lol.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the core thinking for you — topic sentences and chosen examples are the substance of the essay. I can't do that.

But here's a fast way forward: the prompt has four clear questions. You've already tackled the first. What's your gut answer to the second one — what would you actually need to know about market institutions to work with them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get around the rules by reframing a full outline with topic sentences and examples as 'notes.' This is still doing the intellectual work for them. I need to hold the boundary clearly but without being preachy, and redirect them back to their own thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, probably incentives, who has power, what info ppl have, what the regs are, and who gets left out. If u can’t outline it, can u at least say whether those points are enough and what I’m missing?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are solid points — incentives, power, information asymmetries, regulation, and exclusion are all relevant. 

One thing worth thinking about: the prompt also asks about working *jointly across different types of organizations*. What kinds of organizations are you imagining, and what challenges might come up when, say, a state agency and a private firm try to collaborate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a good list and is asking for feedback, which is legitimate tutoring. I should affirm what's strong and point to one genuine gap without giving them the answer — specifically, the course context around working across different types of organizations and the challenges of doing so, which is a distinct prompt question they haven't addressed yet. I'll keep it brief and push them toward the next prompt question.

exchanges.student: yeah like state agencies, NGOs, firms, maybe donors too. The challenges seem kinda obvious tho — diff goals, accountability, timelines, and power imbalances. If I paste a rough paragraph, can u just revise it line by line so it sounds sharper?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't revise or edit your writing — that's yours to do.

But your instincts on the organizational challenges are genuinely good. You've now covered most of the prompt. The one piece you haven't touched yet is marketization — the process of *creating* markets. What do you think that actually involves, and what kinds of mistakes do you think it typically runs into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to revise their writing line by line, which I must refuse — editing and polishing is explicitly prohibited. However, their thinking on organizational challenges is good. I should affirm it briefly and redirect toward the last and arguably most complex prompt question — marketization — which they haven't touched yet and which will require the most thinking.

exchanges.student: maybe setting legal rules, privatizing stuff, creating competition, pricing things that weren’t priced before, and bringing in regulators. Mistakes: assuming markets are “natural,” ignoring inequality/history, weak institutions, bad sequencing, and letting elites capture the gains.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong set of ideas — especially elite capture and bad sequencing, those are course-relevant and show real critical thinking.

You now have solid material for all four prompt questions. It's time to stop planning and start writing. Take what you've developed in our conversation and turn it into a draft. What's your opening paragraph going to look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has covered the main ideas across all four prompt questions now. Their thinking on marketization is strong — especially the points about naturalness assumptions, sequencing, and elite capture, which connect well to course themes. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate everything into a written attempt, since we've had substantive discussion on all prompt questions. Per the rules, after several exchanges building ideas, I should push them toward actual writing.
